3-1.q: When properly developed and administered, budgets provide the following advantages to the organization except to
o: promote the efficient allocation of resources.
provide a structure for measuring performance.
ensure that the organization makes a profit.
3-1.e: Even the best budget cannot ensure that an organization will make a profit.

wrongWhy.0: This is one of the benefits of budgeting.
wrongWhy.1: This is one of the benefits of budgeting.
wrongWhy.2: This is one of the benefits of budgeting.

3-1.q: The use of budgetary slack does not allow the preparer to
o: Increase the probability of achieving budgeted performance.
Use the budget to control subordinate performance.
3-1.e: When a budget is easily achieved, it is said to have budgetary slack in it. When budgetary slack exists, either revenues are understated or expenses are overstated, or both. A budget should be able to be used as a control tool to compare actual performance results with budgeted performance. When the budget is too easily achieved, though, it is difficult for managers to properly evaluate the performance of subordinates. Instead, the budget should set the performance standards at a high but attainable level.

wrongWhy.0: The use of budgetary slack does increase the probability of achieving budgeted performance. Budgetary slack exists when revenues are understated or expenses are overstated. Thus, it is easy to attain budgeted performance. Actual revenues will probably be higher than budgeted revenues and actual expenses will probably be lower than budgeted expenses, so actual net income will probably be higher than budgeted net income.
wrongWhy.1: Budgetary slack exists when revenues are understated or expenses are overstated. Building in budgetary slack does not prevent the manager from making a projection of actual expenses. The manager can and no doubt will make a projection of expenses. However, instead of using his or her projection of expenses as the budgeted expenses, the preparer would submit a higher budgeted expense amount. In that way, the preparer would be attempting to "guarantee" that actual expenses would come in below the budgeted amount.
wrongWhy.2: Budgetary slack exists when revenues are understated or expenses are overstated. The use of budgetary slack does allow the preparer to be flexible under unexpected circumstances because, for example, if actual expenses turn out to be higher than the manager expected, actual expenses may still be less than the budgeted expenses.

3-1.q: A continuous profit plan
o: Is an annual plan that is part of a 5-year plan.
Is a plan that is revised monthly or quarterly.
Works best for a company that can reliably forecast events a year or more into the future.
Is a plan devised by a full-time planning staff.
3-1.e: A continuous plan is one that is automatically prepared for a certain period of time ahead of the present. For example, a 1-year continuous plan will be prepared at the end of every month for the next 12 months.

wrongWhy.0: A continuous plan does not need to be part of a larger 5-year plan.
wrongWhy.2: A continuous plan works for any company, whether or not they can accurately forecast more than a year into the future.
wrongWhy.3: A continuous plan may or may not be produced by a full-time planning staff.

3-1.q: In developing the budget for the next year, which one of the following approaches would produce the greatest amount of positive motivation and goal congruence?
o: Permit the divisional manager to develop the goal for the division that in the manager's view will generate the greatest amount of profits.
Have the divisional and senior management jointly develop goals and objectives while constructing the corporation's overall plan of operation.
Have the divisional and senior management jointly develop goals and the divisional manager develop the implementation plan.
Have senior management develop the overall goals and permit the divisional manager to determine how these goals will be met.
3-1.e: With open communication between the division manager and senior management all of the options can be discussed and a mutually acceptable budget can be developed. Once this budget is developed, the implementation is best left to the division manager because the division manager understands the process, employees, and environment better than senior management can, as senior management is not actively involved in the day to day operations.

wrongWhy.0: While the manager may view his budget for the division as generating the most profit, generally he/she is unaware of what is going on elsewhere within the organization. Without this knowledge, the division manager may be headed in a different direction than what is necessary for organization wide success.
wrongWhy.1: Senior management should prepare the corporation's overall plan prior to budget development. The corporate plan is likely to be longer term and include elements both external and non-financial that a budget does not address.
wrongWhy.3: When senior management develops goals they can miss opportunities or issues known by the division manager. Rarely does one entity fully understand everything that happens with other entities. Communication is essential.

3-1.q: A continuous (rolling) budget
o: Presents the plan for only one level of activity and does not adjust to changes in the level of activity.
Drops the current month or quarter and adds a future month or quarter as the current month or quarter is completed.
Presents planned activities for a period but does not present a firm commitment.
Presents the plan for a range of activity so that the plan can be adjusted for changes in activity.
3-1.e: A continuous budget, also called a rolling budget, is one that is prepared for a certain period of time ahead of the present. For example, a 1-year continuous budget will be prepared at the end of every month for the next 12 months.

wrongWhy.0: This is not the description of a rolling budget. "Continuous" or "rolling" merely identifies a budget that has been prepared for a certain period of time ahead of the present. For example, a 1-year continuous budget will be prepared at the end of every month for the next 12 months. This is a description of a static budget. A static budget is developed for one specific activity level. A static budget may be in the form of a rolling budget, but it may also be an annual budget, that is, a budget prepared for a full fiscal year prior to the beginning of that fiscal year.
wrongWhy.2: This is not the description of a rolling budget. "Continuous" or "rolling" merely identifies a budget that has been prepared for a certain period of time ahead of the present. For example, a 1-year continuous budget will be prepared at the end of every month for the next 12 months. No type of budget should present a firm commitment. A budget should be used as a guide, a motivating device, and a control tool; but it should be able to be revised if necessary. If conditions change during the budget period, the budget should not be used
wrongWhy.3: This is not the description of a rolling budget. "Continuous" or "rolling" merely identifies a budget that has been prepared for a certain period of time ahead of the present. For example, a 1-year continuous budget will be prepared at the end of every month for the next 12 months. No type of budgeting presents the plan for a range of activity so the plan can be adjusted for changes in activity, but this is close to the description of a flexible budget. A flexible budget takes the variable revenues and costs as they are planned in the master budget (the static budget) and adjusts the master budget amounts to what the budgeted amounts would have been if the actual activity levels had been used in preparing the budget. A flexible budget can be prepared only after the actual level of activity is known. A flexible budget may be in the form of a rolling budget, but it may also be an annual budget, that is, a budget prepared for a full fiscal year prior to the beginning of that fiscal year.

3-1.q: All of the following are advantages of top-down budgeting as opposed to participatory budgeting, except that it:
o: May limit the acceptance of proposed goals and objectives.
Reduces the time required for budgeting.
3-1.e: This is not an advantage of top-down budgeting, although it is a true statement. When senior management determines the budget without input from the departments being held to that budget, employees are less likely to feel they can / want to achieve the target. It wasn't their idea and no one listened to them.

wrongWhy.1: This is an advantage of top-town budgeting. When the budget is dictated from above, departmental managers do not need to spend time putting together their plans and ideas for the future. Furthermore, there is no need for lower level managers to make adjustments to the first draft of the budget, as is done with bottom up budgeting after senior management has examined the first draft budgets of the lower level managers. The result is that less time is required for the budgeting process.
wrongWhy.2: This is an advantage of top-down budgeting. Frequently, departmental managers have blinders on and are unaware of what is happening in other areas of the company. Senior management has a better perspective of the big picture and what will be necessary to achieve overall organizational objectives.
wrongWhy.3: This is an advantage of top-town budgeting. Strategic plans are long-term in nature and require a good deal or coordination in order to be successful. Senior management's job is to position each division and department appropriately to fulfill their role in the long term organizational plan. Top-down budgeting achieves this more readily than bottom-up budgeting, because senior management has the required long-term perspective and lower level managers do not.

3-1.q: An advantage of participatory budgeting is that it
o: reduces the effect on the budgetary process of employee biases.
yields information known to management but not to employees.
minimizes the cost of developing budgets.
encourages acceptance of the budget by employees.
3-1.e: Participative (bottom-up) budget development involves all the people affected by the budget, even lower-level employees, and it involves negotiation between lower-level managers and senior management. A participative budget is more likely to gain employee commitment to fulfill budgetary goals than one where the affected people have no input. People are more willing to devote extra effort to attain goals they perceive as their own.

wrongWhy.0: Participative (bottom-up) budget development does not reduce the effect on the budgetary process of employee biases. Participative budget development involves all the people affected by the budget, even lower-level employees, and it involves negotiation between lower-level managers and senior management. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.1: Participative (bottom-up) budget development does not yield information known to management but not to employees. Bottom-up budget development involves all the people affected by the budget, even lower-level employees, and it involves negotiation between lower-level managers and senior management. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: Participative (bottom-up) budget development does not minimize the cost of developing budgets. In fact, participative budgeting is more time-consuming than authoritative budgeting because lower-level managers and employees need to meet and negotiate their budgets, so the cost of participative learning may be greater. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

3-1.q: The goals and objectives upon which an annual profit plan is most effectively based are
o: A combination of financial, quantitative, and qualitative measures.
Financial measures such as net income, return on investment, and earnings per share.
Quantitative measures such as growth in unit sales, number of employees, and manufacturing capacity.
Qualitative measures of organizational activity such as product innovation leadership, product quality levels, and product safety.
3-1.e: An annual profit plan should be based on a combination of financial, quantitative and qualitative measures. The development of goals and objectives is the first step in the planning process. Top management must establish the major goals and objectives, set priorities and communicate these priorities to the people within the organization. Lower levels of the organization bear a part of the responsibility for the overall organizational goals and objectives, which become subunits' goals and objectives. However, specific departments' goals and objectives may conflict with other departments' goals and objectives. For example, a goal of an increase in market size may conflict with a goal of profitability of sales. Thus, profit plans should be based on multiple measures.

wrongWhy.1: An annual profit plan should be based on a combination of financial, quantitative, qualitative measures. This answer includes only financial measures.
wrongWhy.2: An annual profit plan should be based on a combination of financial, quantitative, qualitative measures. This answer includes only quantitative measures.
wrongWhy.3: An annual profit plan should be based on a combination of financial, quantitative, qualitative measures. This answer includes only qualitative measures.

foster the planning of operations, provide a framework for performance evaluation, and promote communication and coordination among the organization’s segments.
foster the planning of operations, facilitate the identification of blame for missed budget predictions, and ensure goal congruence between superiors and subordinates.
define responsibility centers, provide a framework for performance evaluation, and promote communication and coordination among the organization’s segments.

wrongWhy.0: Defining responsibility centers and facilitating identification of blame for missed budget predictions are not objectives of budgeting.
wrongWhy.2: Facilitating the identification of blame for missed budget predictions is not one of the objectives of budgeting.
wrongWhy.3: Defining responsibility centers is not one of the objectives of budgeting.

3-1.q: The following sequence of steps are employed by a company to develop its annual profit plan. Planning guidelines are disseminated downward by top management after receiving input from all levels of management. A sales budget is prepared by individual sales units reflecting the sales targets of the various segments. This provides the basis for departmental production budgets and other related components by the various operating units. Communication is primarily lateral with some upward communication possible. A profit plan is submitted to top management for coordination and review. Top management's recommendations and revisions are acted upon by middle management. A revised profit plan is resubmitted for further review to top management. Top management grants final approval and distributes the formal plan downward to the various operating units. This outline of steps best describes which one of the following approaches to budget development?
o: Total justification of all activities by operating units.
3-1.e: While senior management provides guidelines, the bulk of the budget is developed from input from individual departments. After submission to top management for review, top management's recommendations and revisions are made at the individual department level, and the individual department managers prepare and resubmit revised budget data to top management. This continues until the desired result is achieved for the consolidated budget.

wrongWhy.1: Management did not provide the budgeted numbers, just made recommendations for revisions to the budgets submitted by the various departments.
wrongWhy.2: This would describe zero based budgeting and is not described by the activities listed above. With zero based budgeting, every line item is analyzed and justified in a time consuming process. The guidelines developed from managerial input do not indicate such a thorough analysis.
wrongWhy.3: Management did not dictate the budget, just provided guidelines to give each department an idea of where the company should be headed.

3-1.q: All of the following are disadvantages of top-down budgeting as opposed to participatory budgeting, except that it:

Reduces the time required for budgeting.

Reduces the communication between employees and management.

When senior management dictates the budget, lower level managers and their staff may not only not accept the budget, but they may also not accept senior management’s goals and objectives on which the budget is based. 3-1.q: Budgetary slack can best be described as
o: The planned overestimation of budgeted expenses.
A plug number used to achieve a pre-set level of operating income.
The elimination of certain expenses to enhance budgeted income.
The planned underestimation of budgeted expenses.
3-1.e: When a budget is easily achieved, it is said to have budgetary slack in it. When budgetary slack exists either revenues are understated or expenses are overstated to which makes it difficult to properly evaluate the performance.

wrongWhy.1: Budgetary slack is not a plug number.
wrongWhy.2: Budgetary slack exists when revenues are understated or expenses are overstated. This is done in order to make it easy for the department, company or individual to meet the budget. The elimination of certain items is not budgetary slack, as it is rather making the budget appear better than the actual results will be.
wrongWhy.3: Budgetary slack exists when revenues are understated or expenses are overstated.

3-1.q: Budgeting problems where departmental managers are repeatedly achieving easy goals or failing to achieve demanding goals can be best minimized by establishing:
o: A policy that allows managers to build slack into the budget.
Better communication whereby managers discuss budget matters daily with their superiors.
Participative budgeting where managers pursue objectives consistent with those set by top management.
3-1.e: This is correct. By allowing managers to participate in the budgeting process with the objectives of senior management in mind, the managers can evaluate the department and find the most optimal, and realistic, means to achieve the targets.

wrongWhy.0: Enacting policies that "control" the budget process can result in resentment and ineffective efforts from departmental management. This is not the best way to generate a realistically challenging budget.
wrongWhy.1: Building slack into a budget is not going to be in the organization’s best interest in the long run.
wrongWhy.2: Budget discussions on a daily basis are a bit excessive and not an efficient way to conduct business. Generally there is a budget "season" where the development of the budget is the primary focus, and daily discussions are not unusual. After budget season; however, the communication between managers and their superiors would likely not focus solely on budget matters, although it would cover variances from the budget.

3-1.q: A controllable expense
o: Is an expense that will remain semivariable in total over the relevant range in a given time period.
Is an expense whose actual amount will not normally differ from the standard (budget) amount.
Is an expected future expense that will be different under various alternatives.
Is one that is directly influenced at a given level of managerial authority within a given time period.
3-1.e: A controllable expense is an expense that is directly influenced at a given level of managerial authority within a given time period. Therefore, that manager is able to control this expense because he or she has the authority to make decisions about how the money will be spent.

wrongWhy.0: This is the definition of a semivariable cost.
wrongWhy.1: Any cost can differ from the standard (budget) amount and usually does.
wrongWhy.2: This is the definition of an incremental (differential) cost.

3-1.q: A company uses participative budgeting. In order to more easily meet budgetary goals, the controller underestimates the amount of revenue and overestimates fixed selling and administrative expenses. This is an example of
3-1.e: This is a description of budgetary slack. Budgetary slack is the practice of underestimating planned revenues and overestimating planned costs to make the overall budgeted profit more achievable. Budgetary slack is the difference between the amount budgeted and the amount the manager actually expects.

wrongWhy.0: This is not a description of budgetary variance. In general, a budget variance is the difference between the actual results and the budgeted amount.
wrongWhy.2: This is not a description of zero-based budgeting. Under zero-based budgeting, the budget is prepared without any reference to, or use of, the current period’s budget or the likely operating results for the current period. Every planned activity must be justified with a cost-benefit analysis. In zero-based budgeting, all of the activities that a department undertakes are identified and then justified. Only revenues and costs from activities that are justified are included in the budget.
wrongWhy.3: This is not a description of flexible budgeting. A flexible budget is a budget that is prepared using the budgeted revenues and costs per unit and the actual level of activity (sales or production, as appropriate). It is essentially what the budget would have been if the company had known what the actual level of sales or production would be in advance and had used that information when it developed the budget.

3-1.q: Each organization plans and budgets its operations for slightly different reasons. Which one of the following is not a significant reason for planning and budgeting?
o: Providing a basis for controlling operations.
Forcing managers to consider expected future trends and conditions.
Checking progress toward the objectives of the organization.
3-1.e: The budget is a realistic plan expressed in quantitative terms. A budget serves as a number of tools: planning, control, motivation and communication tools. However, the budget by itself is not able to ensure profitable operations. "Ensure" means guarantee. A budget, if it is used properly to control operations, can help to increase the probability that operations will be profitable. But it cannot ensure that they will be profitable.

wrongWhy.0: Providing a basis for controlling operations is a reason for developing plans and budgets.
wrongWhy.1: Forcing managers to consider expected future trends and conditions is a reason for developing plans and budgets.
wrongWhy.2: Checking progress toward the objectives of the organization is a reason for developing plans and budgets.

3-1.q: A continuous budget is one that
o: is available for a specified future period by adding a period to the period that just ended.
is created after the organization has been operating for at least one period.
is adjusted throughout the period for changing environmental factors.
uses the prior period's actual results as the current period's budget.
3-1.e: At all times, a continuous budget covers a set number of months, quarters, or years into the future. Each month or quarter, the month or quarter just completed is dropped and a new monthly or quarterly budget is added to the end of the budget.

wrongWhy.1: A continuous budget can be created at any time, not just after the organization has been operating for at least one period.
wrongWhy.2: A continuous budget is not adjusted throughout the period for changing environmental factors.
wrongWhy.3: A continuous budget does not use the prior period’s actual results as the current period’s budget.

3-1.e: The sales budget is the first budget to be prepared when developing the master budget.

3-1.q: All of the following are criticisms of the traditional budgeting process except that it
o: incorporates non-financial measures as well as financial measures into its output.
is not used until the end of the budget period to evaluate performance.
overemphasizes a fixed time horizon such as one year.
makes across-the-board cuts when early budget iterations show that planned expenses are too high.
3-1.e: This is not a criticism of the traditional budgeting process or of any budgeting process. A budget needs to incorporate non-financial measures into its output. For example, expectations regarding units to be manufactured and sold in the coming period, number of employees that will be needed, and new products to be introduced, though non-financial measures, will affect the financial items in the budget.

wrongWhy.1: This is a criticism of traditional budgeting, because this can occur. Actual performance for a period is measured against budgeted performance for that period. While it is necessary to wait until the end of a period to measure the budgetary variance for the whole period, the budget period can be broken into smaller timeframes. A 12 month budget can, and often will, be divided into monthly amounts to allow for current month and year-to-date budget variance reporting throughout the year, so that operational adjustments can be made as necessary.
wrongWhy.2: This is a criticism of traditional budgeting. Those who criticize it question why a budget needs to have a fixed time horizon such as one year. They say that different segments of the same company have different needs for planning. The purpose of a forecast should be to recognize issues that need to be planned for in time to plan for them, and this will be different for every area of the company. So different forecasting horizons are needed for different areas, and they need to be monitored individually. One size does not fit all in an organization today.
wrongWhy.3: This is a criticism of traditional budgeting. The traditional budgeting process may lead to across-theboard cuts when early budget iterations show that planned expenses are too high. However, budgeting should not necessarily require across the board cuts, even when expenses are higher than desired. Cost cutting should be based on what is best for the organization. Frequently cuts based on what is best for the organization will not be equally distributed.

3-1.e: A budget in which each quarter is superseded by the next, encouraging management to think about the upcoming quarter is a rolling budget. A rolling budget is a budget that is prepared for a certain period of time ahead of the present. For example, a one-year continuous, or rolling, budget would be prepared at the end of every month for the next twelve months.

The sales budget is the first operating budget to be prepared in the process of developing the master budget for the year. It incorporates the expected sales in units of each product and each product’s expected selling price. The sales budget is based on the firm’s forecasted sales level, its short- and long-term objectives, and its production capacity. Since it is part of the development of the master budget, the sales budget is developed using just one projected sales level. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.1: A budget in which each quarter is superseded by the next, encouraging management to think about the upcoming quarter is not a flexible budget. A flexible budget is a budget in which the master budget amounts have been adjusted to what the budgeted amounts would have been if the actual activity supporting those amounts had been used in preparing the budget. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: A budget in which each quarter is superseded by the next, encouraging management to think about the upcoming quarter is not a master budget. A master budget is a budget that expresses the operating and financial plans of management for a fiscal year, and each item in it is developed using an assumption that includes just one projected activity level. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

If no other costs are incurred, the direct labor cost per unit is
3-1.e: Under vriable costing, the fixed rental costs are expensed in the period that they are incurred and not allocated to the units produced. Also, the administrtaive costs are not included in the manufacturing costs. Since the total manufacturing costs are $27 per unit and we know that the direct material osts are $16 per unit, there is a remaining $11 of manufacturing costs that will be either variable overheads or direct labor. We are told in the question that no additional costs were incurred other than direct materials, so the direct material cost is $11 per unit.

wrongWhy.1: This answer incorrectly treats the administration expenses as a manufacturing costs under variable costing. Administration costs are not manufacturing costs.
wrongWhy.2: This answer incorrectly treats the fixed rent as part of the manufacturing costs under variable costing. 3-1.q: The budgeting technique that is most likely to motivate managers is
3-1.e: Bottom-up budgeting is the budgeting technique that motivates lower-level managers the most. The budgeting process starts with departments at the lowest level in the organization. Then the budgets are sent on to the next highest level, which does the same thing, and so forth. At the top the company's budget is developed by consolidating all of the lower-level budgets. Of course, top management reviews the lower-level budgets as well as the consolidated budget that results from them. If the company's consolidated budgeted results are not what top management wants to see, top management may negotiate changes to the lower-level budgets as necessary to achieve the company's objectives. Employees are more likely to support budgets when they have participated in their preparation. It gives them a feeling of ownership of the process and they will be more likely to support the budget's implementation.

3-1.q: All of the following are disadvantages of authoritative budgeting as opposed to participatory budgeting, except that it

reduces the communication between employees and management.

Those costs are: (1) Without input from lower level managers, the budget developed by senior management may result in an impractical budget because it does not take into account any existing limitations that senior management may not be aware of. (2) It reduces communication between employees and managers and between middle managers and senior management, since input from mid-level managers is neither sought nor desired by senior management. (3) Without any input into the budgeting process, lower level managers and employees will not see the budget as their own. This may result in a lack of their acceptance of senior management's proposed goals and objectives.

wrongWhy.0: This is a true statement. An authoritative budget is one that is developed by senior management with no input from lower level employees and managers. Without any input into the budgeting process, lower level managers and employees will not see the budget as their own. This may result in a lack of their acceptance of senior management's proposed goals and objectives.
wrongWhy.2: This is a true statement. An authoritative budget is one that is developed by senior management with no input from lower level employees and managers. It reduces communication between employees and managers and between middle managers and senior management, since input from mid-level managers is neither sought nor desired by senior management.
wrongWhy.3: This is a true statement. An authoritative budget is one that is developed by senior management with no input from lower level employees and managers. Without input from lower level employees and managers, a budget may result in an impractical budget that is not achievable, because it does not take into account existing limitations that senior management may not be aware of.

3-1.q: Given the following data for Scurry Company, what is the cost of goods sold? Beginning inventory of finished goods $100,000 Cost of goods manufactured 700,000 Ending inventory of finished goods 200,000 Beginning work-in-process inventory 300,000 Ending work-in-process inventory 50,000

3-1.q: From the perspective of corporate management, the use of budgetary slack
o: Increases the effectiveness of the corporate planning process.
Increases the ability to identify potential budget weaknesses.
Increases the probability that budgets will not be achieved.
Increases the likelihood of inefficient resource allocation.
3-1.e: The use of budgetary slack increases the likelihood of inefficient resource allocation. When a budget is easily achieved, it is said to have budgetary slack in it. When budgetary slack exists, either budgeted revenues are understated or budgeted expenses are overstated, or both. Hence, management won't work hard on increasing revenues or minimizing costs as they simply have to achieve easily attainable goals. Thus, the resources most likely will be allocated inefficiently.

wrongWhy.0: The use of budgetary slack does not increase the effectiveness of the corporate planning process. Budgetary slack exists when budgeted revenues are understated or budgeted expenses are overstated, or both. Budgets should set high but with attainable performance standards. However, budgetary slack makes it easy to achieve the budgeted level of performance. Thus, the effectiveness of the corporate planning process decreases when there is budgetary slack.
wrongWhy.1: The use of budgetary slack does not increase the ability to identify potential budget weaknesses. Budgetary slack exists when budgeted revenues are understated or budgeted expenses are overstated. Since the budgetary slack distorts a real attainable level of performance it is difficult to identify potential budget weaknesses.
wrongWhy.2: The use of budgetary slack does not increase the probability that budgets will not be achieved--just the opposite. When a budget is easily achieved, it is said to have budgetary slack in it. When budgetary slack exists, either budgeted revenues are understated or budgeted expenses are overstated, or both. Thus, budgetary slack increases the probability that budgets will be achieved.

3-2.q: Barnes Corporation expected to sell 150,000 board games during the month of November, and the company's master budget contained the following data related to the sale and production of these games: Revenue $2,400,000 Direct materials 675,000 Direct labor 300,000 Variable overhead 450,000 Contribution Fixed overhead 250,000 Fixed selling/administration 500,000 Operating income $ 975,000 $ 225,000 Actual sales during November were 180,000 games. Using a flexible budget, the company expects the operating income for the month of November to be
3-2.e: The flexible budget is the budget developed for the actual achieved level of activity rather than the master budget level. To compute the flexible budget we must use the standard costing system, i.e. we need to determine the budgeted selling price, budgeted variable cost per unit, and budgeted total amount of fixed costs. The budgeted selling price minus the budgeted cost per unit equal the contribution per unit, so we can use the budgeted contribution per unit and need not calculate the first two items. The budgeted contribution per unit is $6.50 ($975,000 ÷ 150,000). Fixed costs for the flexible budget are the same as for the static budget, since fixed costs do not fluctuate with the level of output.

wrongWhy.1: This answer results from incorrectly treating variable overhead costs as fixed costs. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.
wrongWhy.2: This is the static budget operating income not adjusted for the actual level of output.
wrongWhy.3: This is calculated using the static budget operating income per unit of $1.50 ($225,000 ÷ 150,000) and multiplied by the actual level of sales of 180,000. This gives the operating income of $270,000 ($1.5 × 180,000), but it ignores the fact that fixed costs do not vary with the level of activity.

3-2.q: A budget developed by using budgeted prices and budgeted costs based on the actual output in the budget period is a(n)
3-2.e: A flexible budget takes the variable revenues and costs as they are planned in the master budget (the static budget) and adjusts the master budget amounts to what the budgeted amounts would have been if the actual activity level had been used in preparing the budget. Thus, a flexible budget is developed by using budgeted prices and budgeted costs based on the actual output in the budget period.

wrongWhy.0: A budget developed by using budgeted prices and budgeted costs based on the actual output in the budget period is not an operating budget. Operating budgets are used to identify the resources that will be needed to carry out the planned activities during the budget period, such as sales, services, production, purchasing, marketing, and R&D (research and development). The operating budgets for individual units are compiled into the budgeted income statement. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: A budget developed by using budgeted prices and budgeted costs based on the actual output in the budget period is not a life-cycle budget. A life-cycle budget is used to plan incomes and expenses for one specific product throughout its entire life cycle, from its development through its decline, enabling the company to see the cash flows that will result from the product over its entire life. When all the lifetime development and production costs are set forth in the life-cycle budget, management can set a price that will cover not only the company’s costs but also its required return on investment. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: A budget developed by using budgeted prices and budgeted costs based on the actual output in the budget period is not a continuous budget. A continuous budget, also called a rolling budget, is one that is prepared for a certain period of time ahead of the present. For example, a one-year continuous budget would be prepared at the end of every month or possibly quarter for the next twelve months. Each month or quarter, the month or quarter just completed is dropped from the budget and a new monthly or quarterly budget is added to the end of the budget. At the same time as a month or quarter is dropped and a new month or quarter is added, the other periods in the budget can be revised to reflect any new information that has become available. Thus, the budget is continuously being updated and always covers the same amount of time in the future. For example, in September 20X3, a monthly rolling budget will cover the months of October 20X3 through September 20X4. In October 20X3, the rolling budget will cover the months of November 20X3 through October 20X4. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

3-2.q: A budgeting approach that requires a manager to justify the entire budget for each budget period is known as:
3-2.e: Zero-based budgeting is a budgeting method in which the budget is prepared without any reference to, or use of, the current period’s budget and the likely operating results for the current period. In zero-based budgeting the manager must start from scratch and justify all incomes and expenses proposed.

wrongWhy.0: Incremental budgeting simply takes historical information and adjusts it for anticipated increases or decreases in the coming year. This type of budgeting does not involve justifying the entire budget for each budget period.
wrongWhy.2: Performance budgets use statements of missions, goals and objectives to explain why the money is being spent. It is a way to allocate resources to achieve specific objectives based on program goals and measured results. Performance budgeting starts with a goal, then determines strategies and activities to accomplish that goal and justify the expenses. However, that justification is limited to the activities that accomplish a particular goal and does not extend to the entire budget.
wrongWhy.3: Program budgeting is used mainly by non-profit organizations and governmental bodies. It involves planning and budgeting for a specific program which may have not only expenses but income as well. If the program has income, the goal is to have the program income not only cover the program expenses but to exceed them. Program budgeting is limited to the activities that relate to one specific program and does not extend to the entire budget.

3-2.q: There are many different budget techniques or processes that business organizations can employ. One of these techniques or processes is zero-base budgeting, which is:
o: Using the prior year's budget as a base year and adjusting it based on the experiences of the prior year and the expectations for the coming year.

Developing budgeted costs from clear-cut measured relationships between inputs and outputs.
Budgeting for cash inflows and outflows to time investments and borrowings in a way to maintain a bank account with a minimum balance.
3-2.e: Zero-based budgeting is the budgeting method in which the current year budget is prepared without any reference to, or use of, the prior period's budget. Within zero-based budgeting all of the activities that a department undertakes are identified and then prioritized. This is the manner in which costs are justified and supported. Also, because the manager needs to examine every single expenditure and activity within the department, he is more likely to develop an alternative and, hopefully cheaper, method of accomplishing the same thing.

wrongWhy.0: This is not the definition of zero-based budgeting. This is a description of incremental budgeting.
wrongWhy.2: This is not the definition of zero-based budgeting.
wrongWhy.3: This is not the definition of zero-based budgeting. This describes cash management.

3-2.e: Zero-based budgeting is a budgeting system in which every year the company starts with zero for their budget. This means that all costs need to be justified every year and all revenues need to be specifically planned for. This is the method that is being used in this question.

3-2.q: The use of standard costs in the budgeting process signifies that an organization has most likely implemented a
3-2.e: A flexible budget is a budget that is prepared for the actual activity level achieved during the period. This is done using the standard cost per unit. Therefore, if a company is using standard costs it may indicate that they are also using a flexible budget.

wrongWhy.0: A fixed budget, or static budget, is a budget that is prepared for only one level of activity within the company. A static budget does not require the use of standard costs.
wrongWhy.2: Zero-based budgeting is the budgeting method in which the current year budget is prepared without any reference to, or use of, the prior period's budget. The use of this form of budgeting does not require the use of standard costs.
wrongWhy.3: The capital budget is the budget in which all capital (property, plant and equipment) expenditures are planned. This budget is often prepared years in advance so that the company is able to obtain the necessary financing or accumulate the necessary cash to carry out its capital expansion plans. Although capital expansion plans that affect the budget being developed must be incorporated into that budget, there is no reason to say that use of standard costs in the budgeting process signifies that an organization has implemented a capital budget. To the extent that the capital budget is incorporated into the budget being developed, a capital budget is a part of any budget, whether it is a static budget, a flexible budget, or a zero-base budget.

3-2.q: Based on past experience, a company has developed the following budget formula for estimating its shipping expenses. 3-2.e: A lot of information is given in this question, but much of it is not needed. 3-2.q: A manufacturing firm has certain peak seasons, the summer season and the last two weeks of February. During these periods of increased output, the firm leases additional production equipment and hires additional temporary employees. Which one of the following budget techniques would best fit this firm’s needs?
o: Project budgeting because discrete financial information is available.
Static budgeting because it shows the changes in sales or production levels.
Zero-based budgeting because it is based on manager input.
Flexible budgeting because it allows for adjustments to the budget based on actual activity levels.
3-2.e: Flexible budgeting is the budget technique that would best fit this firm’s needs. A flexible budget is prepared for the actual level of activity using all of the budgeted variable costs per unit along with the budgeted total fixed cost as determined at the beginning of the year. The flexible budget is the budget that would have been prepared for the actual level of activity for the period if the actual level of activity had been known in advance. Thus, flexible budgeting can adapt to changes in activity.

wrongWhy.0: Project budgeting is not the budget technique that would best fit this firm’s needs. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.1: Static budgeting is not the budget technique that would best fit this firm’s needs. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: Zero-based budgeting is not the budget technique that would best fit this firm’s needs. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

3-2.q: Which one of the following statements regarding the difference between a flexible budget and a static budget is correct?
o: A flexible budget is established by operating management, while a static budget is determined by top management.
A flexible budget usually includes budgeted variable revenue and cost amounts for a different level of activity from the level of activity used in the static budget.
A flexible budget includes only variable costs, whereas a static budget includes only fixed costs.
A flexible budget primarily is prepared for planning purposes, while a static budget is prepared for performance evaluation.
3-2.e: The static budget is based on the level of activity planned at the start of the budget period. A flexible budget is developed using the budgeted revenues and costs per unit of activity as developed for the static budget, but based on the level of actual activity achieved in the budget period. The major difference between a flexible budget and a static budget is the use of the actual activity level in the flexible budget, whereas the static budget uses the activity level planned at the beginning of the budget period.

wrongWhy.0: There are a number of methods of developing budgets: participative budgeting, bottomup budgeting, top-down approach, etc. Any level of management can establish either a flexible budget or a static budget or both.
wrongWhy.2: Flexible budgets and static budgets both include both variable and fixed costs.
wrongWhy.3: A flexible budget and a static budget are usually both prepared for planning and performance evaluation purposes.

3-2.q: The controller of Allenwood Steel Company has tasked the newly-hired budget manager with restructuring the company’s budget system. The controller would like her to gather information from each unit on their activities, and, with top management, examine each unit’s contributions to the company as a whole. Then, with the objective of eliminating inefficiency and waste, each unit’s budget for the following year will be determined without regard to past budgets and with little regard for past operating results. This method of budget development is called
3-2.e: Zero-based budgeting involves preparing the budget without any reference to, or use of, the current period’s budget or the likely operating results for the current period. Every planned activity must be justified with a costbenefit analysis. Zero-based budgeting is in contrast to incremental budgeting, where budgets are developed by beginning with the current period’s actual or the current period’s budgeted figures and adjusting them for any changes anticipated in the coming period.

wrongWhy.0: Determining each unit’s budget for the following year without regard to past budgets and with little regard for past operating results is not called activity-based budgeting. wrongWhy.1: Determining each unit’s budget for the following year without regard to past budgets and with little regard for past operating results is not called pro forma budgeting. There is no such thing as "pro forma budgeting." Pro forma financial statements are prepared for internal use in the planning process and contain projected amounts that are expected if a particular course of action is followed. Pro forma financial statements are used in order to see what the financial statements of the firm will look like if something under consideration or forecasted actually happens. The term “pro forma” is sometimes used to refer to the master budget financial statements, but use in that context is an inappropriate use of the term. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.3: Determining each unit’s budget for the following year without regard to past budgets and with little regard for past operating results is not called flexible budgeting. A flexible budget is based on the master budget, but it adjusts the master budget amounts to what the budgeted amounts would have been if the actual activity supporting those amounts had been used in preparing the budget. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

3-2.e: This is a description of a static budget because the static budget is prepared for only one level of activity (a certain level of sales).

3-2.q: Flexible budgets
o: Are static budgets that have been revised for changes in prices.
Accommodate changes in the inflation rate.
Are used to evaluate capacity use.
3-2.e: A flexible budget is based on the static budget, but variable revenues and variable expenses are adjusted upward or downward to reflect the actual activity level. A flexible budget is more useful than a static budget because it allows the company to compare the actual results with the budgeted results that have been adjusted for the actual level of activity. Thus, it accommodates changes in activity levels.

3-2.q: The major feature of zero-based budgeting is that it
o: evaluates each activity and determines whether it should be maintained as it is, reduced, or eliminated.
focuses on planned capital outlays for property, plant, and equipment.
uses the previous year's budget and adjusts it for inflation.
assumes all activities are legitimate and worthy of receiving budget increases to cover any increased costs.
3-2.e: Under zero-based budgeting, the budget is prepared without any reference to, or use of, the current period’s budget or the likely operating results for the current period. Every planned activity must be justified with a cost-benefit analysis. Because the budget is built up from zero, each manager must justify all of the expenses in his or her department. Zero-based budgeting enables the company to identify expenses that are not value adding or that should be reduced due to some development in production methods or something similar.

wrongWhy.3: Zero-based budgeting does not assume that all activities are legitimate and worthy of receiving budget increases to cover any increased costs. Zero-based budgeting is just the opposite: every planned activity must be justified with a cost-benefit analysis.

3-2.q: Country Ovens is a family restaurant chain. Due to an unexpected road construction project, traffic passing by the Country Ovens restaurant in Newtown has significantly increased. As a result, restaurant volume has similarly increased well beyond the level expected. Which type of budget would be most appropriate in helping the restaurant manager plan for restaurant labor costs?
3-2.e: A flexible budget would be the most appropriate budget, as it would allow management to measure operating success at any activity level experienced. A flexible budget can be adjusted upward for increased traffic and, if necessary, adjusted back down when the construction is completed, providing management with accurate budget variance information under a variety of circumstances.

wrongWhy.0: Zero-based budgeting is a budgeting method in which the budget is prepared without any reference to, or use of, the current period’s budget and the likely operating results for the current period. Although this approach would be appropriate when the next year's volume is expected to far exceed this year’s sales volume, it is still a static budget. And a static budget does not make allowance for changes in volume that may take place. Therefore, this would not be the best choice in this situation.
wrongWhy.1: A rolling budget is a continual budget that adds one month as another month is completed. This type of budget is a static budget, and a static budget does not make allowance for changes in volume that may take place. Therefore, this would not be the best choice in this situation.
wrongWhy.3: Activity-based budgeting is similar in concept to activity-based costing. Activities that drive the costs are identified, a budgeted level of activity for each of these drivers is determined based on a budgeted level of production, and budgeted amounts are developed based on the budgeted level of activity. This is a good basis for developing a budget, but since it depends upon a budgeted level of activity, the budget developed will be a static budget that will not make allowance for changes in volume that may take place. Therefore, this would not be the best choice in this situation.

is comprised of the budgeted income statement and its supporting schedules for a budget period.

3-2.q: A budget that expresses the operating and financial plans of management for a fiscal year is called a
3-2.e: The master budget expresses the operating and financial plans of management for a fiscal year. The master budget is a full set of budgeted financial statements for the budget year, including monthly or at least quarterly interim budgeted financial statements. The budgeted financial statements include the budgeted balance sheet, budgeted income statement, and budgeted statement of cash flows. The master budget is created using both non-financial and financial assumptions, which come about as a result of the planning process. The master budget is a result of both operating decisions and financing decisions. Operating decisions are concerned with the best use of the company’s limited resources. Financing decisions are concerned with obtaining the funds to acquire the resources the company needs. The master budget is a static budget because each item in it is developed using an assumption that includes just one projected activity level.

wrongWhy.0: A budget that expresses the operating and financial plans of management for a fiscal year is not called a rolling budget. A rolling budget is a budget that is prepared for a certain period of time ahead of the present. For example, a one-year continuous, or rolling, budget would be prepared at the end of every month for the next twelve months. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.1: A budget that expresses the operating and financial plans of management for a fiscal year is not called a flexible budget. A flexible budget is based on the master budget, but it adjusts the master budget amounts to what the budgeted amounts would have been if the actual activity supporting those amounts had been used in preparing the budget. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.
wrongWhy.2: A budget that expresses the operating and financial plans of management for a fiscal year is not called a strategic budget. A strategic budget is a budget that is aligned with and takes into consideration the entity's long term objectives that will take more than one year to achieve. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

3-2.q: In an organization that plans by using comprehensive budgeting, the master budget refers to:
o: the booklet containing budget guidelines, policies, and forms to use in the budgeting process.
a budget of a not-for-profit organization after it is approved by the appropriate authoritative body.
a compilation of all the separate operational and financial budget schedules of the organization.
the current budget updated for operations for part of the current year.

wrongWhy.0: This does not describe the master budget.
wrongWhy.1: This does not describe the master budget.
wrongWhy.3: This does not describe the master budget.

3-2.q: A systemized approach known as zero-base budgeting (ZBB)
o: Divides the activities of individual responsibility centers into a series of packages that are prioritized.
Presents the plan for only one level of activity and does not adjust to changes in the level of activity.
Presents a statement of expectations for a period of time but does not present a firm commitment.
Focuses on the budgeted cost of activities necessary to produce products.
3-2.e: Zero-based budgeting is the budgeting method in which the current year budget is prepared without any reference to, or use of, the prior period's budget. This is quite different from a budget that is prepared by looking at the current year's actual or budgeted amounts and simply adjusting them (usually increasing them) for expected changes in the coming year. Though this method is more time consuming and difficult for all of the people involved, there are a number of advantages to the company as a result of using this method. Because the budget is built up from zero, each manager must justify all of the expenses in his or her department. Each component is evaluated from a cost-benefit perspective and priorities are determined. Zero-based budgeting enables the company to identify expenses that are not value adding or that should be reduced due to some development in production methods or something similar.

wrongWhy.1: This is a description of a static budget, not a zero-base budget. A static budget is a budget that is prepared for only one level of activity within the company. A zero-base budget may be a static budget, but it may also be a flexible budget.
wrongWhy.2: Zero-based budgeting represents a firm commitment to the company just like any other budget.
wrongWhy.3: Zero-based budgeting is applicable to all areas of the organization and includes all selling and administrative costs, not only costs for activities to produce products.

3-2.q: A flexible budget is appropriate for
o: Control of direct materials and direct labor but not selling and administrative expenses.
Control of fixed factory overhead but not direct materials and direct labor.

However, a flexible budget is necessary to control direct materials and direct labor if the actual activity level differs from the static budget activity level.

To control fixed costs, the use of static budget is appropriate, not necessarily the use of flexible budget.
wrongWhy.1: While a flexible budget can be prepared for any level of activity, this is not the best answer to the question among the answer choices given.

A flexible budget is necessary to control direct materials and direct labor if the actual activity level differs from static budget activity level.

3-3.q: When sales volume is seasonal in nature, certain items in the budget must be coordinated. The three most significant items to coordinate in budgeting seasonal sales volume are
o: Raw material inventory, direct labor hours, and manufacturing overhead costs.
Raw material inventory, work-in-process inventory, and production volume.
Production volume, finished goods inventory, and sales volume.
Direct labor hours, work-in-process inventory, and sales volume.
3-3.e: Budgets usually start with the development of the sales budget, and it is the most difficult budget to prepare. Production volume and finished goods inventory budgets are the next budgets to be prepared, and their development is based on the assumptions made in preparing the sales budget. When the business is seasonal in nature it is even more difficult to predict sales volume and subsequent budgets. Thus, for any type of business including seasonal it is critical to coordinate production volume, finished goods inventory, and sales volume first of all.

wrongWhy.0: The sales budget is usually the first thing to determine in the budgeting process of any type of business including a seasonal business. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.
wrongWhy.1: The sales budget is usually the first thing to determine in the budgeting process of any type of business including a seasonal business. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.
wrongWhy.3: Direct labor hours, work-in-process inventory are elements within the production budget. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.

3-3.q: Myers Company uses a calendar-year and prepares a cash budget for each month of the year. Which one of the following items should be considered when developing July's cash budget?
o: Property taxes levied in the last calendar year scheduled to be paid quarterly in the coming year during the last month of each calendar quarter.
Quarterly cash dividends scheduled to be declared on July 15 and paid on August 6 to shareholders of record as of July 25.
Federal income tax and social security tax withheld from employees' June paychecks to be remitted to the Internal Revenue Service in July.
Recognition that 0.5% of the July sales on account will be credit losses.
3-3.e: The payment of federal income tax and social security tax withheld from employees' June paychecks to be remitted to the Internal Revenue Service in July will affect July cash because it is a July disbursement to be made. Therefore, it should be considered when developing July’s cash budget.

wrongWhy.0: The payments of property taxes will be made during the last month of each calendar quarter. The last months of each calendar quarter are March, June, September and December. Since a quarterly property tax payment will not be made during July, it should not be considered when developing July's cash budget.
wrongWhy.1: The quarterly cash dividend will be accrued in July. However, it will not be paid until August, so it will not affect cash during July. Therefore, it should not be considered when developing July's cash budget.
wrongWhy.3: The allowance for credit losses is recorded by debiting credit loss expense and crediting the allowance for credit losses account. Since it is not a cash transaction, it should not be considered when developing July's cash budget.

3-3.q: When budgeting, the items to be considered by a manufacturing firm in going from a sales quantity budget to a production budget would be the

Furthermore, inventory levels must be a consideration in developing the production budget. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

3-3.q: Playtime Toys estimates that it will sell 200,000 dolls during the coming year. The beginning inventory is 12,000 dolls; the target ending inventory is 15,000 dolls. Each doll requires two shoes which are purchased from an outside supplier. The beginning inventory of shoes is 20,000; the target ending inventory is 18,000 shoes. The number of shoes that should be purchased during the year is
3-3.e: The question tells us that beginning finished goods inventory of dolls on hand is 12,000 dolls and the target ending units of finished dolls in inventory is 15,000 dolls. The company plans to sell 200,000 dolls. The inventory formula is: Beginning Inventory + Units Produced/Purchased − Units Sold/Used = Ending Inventory Letting P stand for Units Produced, we have the following formula: Beginning Inventory of 12,000 finished dolls + P − 200,000 dolls sold = Ending Inventory of 15,000 dolls. Solving for P, we get P = 203,000 dolls. Since each doll produced requires 2 shoes, the total shoes required for production of 203,000 dolls = 203,000 × 2 = 406,000 shoes. We now use the shoes needed for current production along with the beginning and ending inventories of shoes to calculate the number of shoes that will need to be purchased. We use the same inventory formula: Beginning Inventory + Units Produced/Purchased − Units Sold/Used = Ending Inventory. Letting P stand for Purchases of shoes, we have the following formula: Beginning Inventory of 20,000 shoes + P − 406,000 shoes Used in Production = Ending Inventory of 18,000 shoes.

wrongWhy.0: This answer results from using the number of dolls to be sold instead of the number to be produced in calculating the number of shoes needed.
wrongWhy.1: This answer results from reversing the beginning and ending inventories when calculating both the number of dolls to be produced and the number of shoes to be purchased.
wrongWhy.2: This answer results from calculating Purchases using the following formula for shoes materials inventory: 20,000 Beginning Inventory + Purchases - 203,000 Used in Production = 18,000 Ending Inventory . . . and solving for Purchases and then doubling the Purchases figure that results. However, the number of shoes used in production is incorrect, as 203,000 is the number of dolls to be produced and each doll requires two shoes. Doubling the Purchases figure that results from that formula does not result in the correct number of shoes to be purchased.

3-3.q: A firm develops an annual cash budget in order to
o: Avoid the opportunity costs of non-invested excess cash and minimize the cost of interim financing.
Determine the opportunity costs of alternative sales and production strategies.
Ascertain which capital expenditure projects are feasible and which capital expenditure projects should be deferred.
Support the preparation of its cash flow statement for the annual report.
3-3.e: The cash budget (or cash management and working capital budget) tracks the inflows and outflows of cash on a month-by-month (possibly even week-by-week or day-by-day) basis. If this budget is accurate it will allow the company to plan for any cash shortfalls that may occur during the year and also enable it to plan for any excess cash accumulating during the year. This can enable the company to plan its short-term investments of excess cash so that they mature when the cash is needed. And prediction of cash shortfalls enables the company to obtain a loan more easily because it is aware of its need before the shortfall arrives, and it can present cash inflow and outflow projections to the bank to support its loan request.

wrongWhy.1: The cash budget does not determine the opportunity costs of alternative sales and production strategies.
wrongWhy.2: The cash budget does not ascertain which capital expenditure projects are feasible or should be deferred. It only shows the cash available for projects and other activities.
wrongWhy.3: The cash flow statement in the annual report is based on actual results, not on budgeted figures.

3-3.q: Swan Company is a maker of men's slacks. The company would like to maintain 20,000 yards of fabric in ending inventory. The beginning fabric inventory is expected to contain 25,000 yards. The expected yards of fabric needed for sales is 90,000. Compute the yards of fabric that Swan needs to purchase.
3-3.e: The basic inventory formula is Beginning Inventory + Amount Purchased/Produced − Amount Sold/Used = Ending Inventory We know beginning inventory, ending inventory and amount used, so we can calculate the amount purchased. Letting P stand for Amount Purchased: 25,000 + P − 90,000 = 20,000 Solving for P, we get P = 85,000.

wrongWhy.0: This is the sum of the beginning inventory, the amount used, and the ending inventory. The basic inventory formula is Beginning Inventory + Amount Purchased/Produced − Amount Sold/Used = Ending Inventory.
wrongWhy.2: This answer results from reversing the beginning and ending inventory amounts in the calculation of the amount purchased. The basic inventory formula is Beginning Inventory + Amount Purchased/Produced − Amount Sold/Used = Ending Inventory.
wrongWhy.3: 90,000 is the amount that Swan expects to use for current sales. It does not include the change in inventory from the beginning of the period to the end of the period. The basic inventory formula is Beginning Inventory + Amount Purchased/Produced − Amount Sold/Used = Ending Inventory.

3-3.q: The preparation of a comprehensive master budget culminates with the preparation of the
o: Cash management and working capital budget.
3-3.e: The cash budget, also called the cash management or working capital budget, is the last budget prepared. This is because it is dependent on all of the other budgets since the production budget, advertising budget, overhead budget, and so on all have a cash component that is reflected in the cash budget. The cash budget is therefore prepared last, because all of the other budgets contribute to it.

wrongWhy.0: There is no such thing as a "strategic budget." The master budget is an annual budget. The quantitative effect of strategic plans, which are long-term plans for periods of more than five years, must be integrated into the annual budgeting process, as well as into the capital budgeting process. As such, strategic planning precedes the development of the annual budget.
wrongWhy.2: The capital investment budget is a long-term budget that is outside of the normal, annual budgeting process. The effect of budgeted current period expenditures for capital assets will need to be taken into account in the development of the master budget, since they will affect the balance sheet, income statement, and cash flows. However, the capital investment budget is not the final budget prepared in preparation of the comprehensive master budget.
wrongWhy.3: The production budget is a part of the operating budget. The operating budget is part of the master budget. The master budget is a comprehensive expression of management's operating and financial plans for a future time period (usually a year) that is summarized in a set of budgeted financial statements. It embraces the impact of both operating and financing decisions.

3-3.q: The Jung Corporation's budget calls for the following production: Qtr 1 : 45,000 units Qtr 2 : 38,000 units Qtr 3 : 34,000 units Qtr 4 : 48,000 units Each unit of product requires three pounds of direct material. The company's policy is to begin each quarter with an inventory of direct materials equal to 30% of that quarter's direct material requirements. Budgeted direct materials purchases for the third quarter would be
3-3.e: This is a basic question of units needed in a period, but it is about the number of units of the raw materials that are needed. There are 3 units of raw materials in a finished unit. The amount needed in the third quarter itself is 102,000 units of raw materials (3 × 34,000 finished units). In addition, the ending inventory is 30% of the next quarter's needs. This is 43,200 units of raw materials (48,000 finished units × 3 × 0.3). The beginning inventory was 30% of the current quarter's needs or 30,600 (102,000 × 0.3). Beginning Inventory + Purchases − Amount Used = Ending Inventory 30,600 + Purchases − 102,000 = 43,200 Purchases = 114,600 A total of 114,600 units of raw materials need to be purchased this period.

wrongWhy.0: This answer results from assuming that each unit of finished product requires only one unit of raw materials. Each unit of finished product requires three units of raw materials.
wrongWhy.1: This is the amount of beginning inventory. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.
wrongWhy.2: This is the ending inventory amount. See the correct answer for a complete explanation.

3-3.e: The direct materials usage budget can be prepared only after the production budget has been prepared. Without knowing how many units will be produced, it is not possible to know how much direct materials will be needed for the production.

Furthermore, the capital expenditures budget is not a part of the annual budget development process. It is usually prepared for years in advance and reviewed on an annual basis; and any capital expenditures to be made during the budget year are included in the budgeting process for the year. Please see the correct answer explanation for more information.

3-3.q: Ming Company has budgeted sales at 6,300 units for the next fiscal year and desires to have 590 good units on hand at the end of that year. Beginning inventory is 470 units. Ming has found from past experience that 10% of all units produced do not pass final inspection and must therefore be destroyed. How many units should Ming plan to produce in the next fiscal year?
3-3.e: The formula for inventory is: Beginning Inventory + Units Produced (or Purchased) − Units Sold = Ending Inventory. If you have any 3 of those numbers, you can calculate the 4th number. Here, we have beginning inventory, sold units, and ending inventory. We also know that 10% of all units produced are not usable. Beginning inventory = 470 Units sold = 6,300 Ending inventory = 590 The calculation of good units to be produced can be accomplished with a simple linear equation, following the inventory formula above: 470 + X − 6,300 = 590 X = 6,420 6,420 is the number of good units that are needed. However, the question asks how many units should be produced, taking into consideration the fact that 10% of the units produced will be defective. The equation to calculate how many units need to be produced in order to result in 6,420 good units after the bad units have been thrown away is: .90X = 6,420 X = 7,133

wrongWhy.1: This covers the sales forecast as well as the desired ending inventory but forgets about the beginning inventory and also the fact that 10% of production will not pass inspection. See the correct answer for a full explanation.
wrongWhy.2: This answer results from incorrectly calculating the total production required in order to compensate for the 10% that do not pass final inspection. To adjust for the defective units, it is necessary to divide the number of good units needed by 0.90 instead of multiplying the number of good units needed by 1.10.

3-3.q: Which one of the following would cause a company’s production budget to decrease?
o: A decrease in required ending inventory.
An increase in beginning direct labor inventory.
A decrease in units produced per direct labor hour.
An increase in direct labor costs per unit.
3-3.e: The production budget is the number of finished goods units to be produced. The number of finished goods units to be produced is: Budgeted sales in units Plus: Ending finished goods inventory required Equals: Total units required Minus: Beginning finished goods inventory Equals: Finished goods units to be produced If the required ending inventory is decreased, the total units required will be decreased and the finished goods units to be produced will be decreased. The result would be a decrease in the production budget.

wrongWhy.1: Labor cannot be inventoried, so there is no such thing as an increase in beginning direct labor inventory.
wrongWhy.2: The production budget is the number of finished goods units to be produced. A decrease in units produced per direct labor hour would cause the direct labor costs budget to increase. It would not cause any change in the number of finished goods units required to be produced and thus it would not cause any change in the production budget.
wrongWhy.3: The production budget is the number of finished goods units to be produced. An increase in direct labor costs per unit would cause the direct labor costs budget to increase. It would not cause any change in the number of finished goods units to be produced and thus it would not cause any change in the production budget.

3-3.q: Which one of following budgets is regarded as the foundation of the master budget?
3-3.e: The operating budget is the budgeted income statement and all the individual budgets that feed into it. The operating budget must be prepared before the financial budget can be prepared. The first operating budget to be prepared is always the sales budget, because the production budget and all the other budgets for the company are derived from the sales budget. Therefore, the foundation of the master budget, which includes both the operating and the financial budgets, is the sales budget because the sales budget is the budget from which all the other budgets are derived.

wrongWhy.0: The cash budget is a component of the financial budget. The cash budget is not the foundation of the master budget. The foundation of the master budget is the budget from which all the other component budgets are derived.
wrongWhy.2: The operating budget is the budgeted income statement and all the individual budgets that feed into it. The operating budget is not the foundation of the master budget, though it is a component of the master budget. The foundation of the master budget is the budget from which all the other component budgets are derived.
wrongWhy.3: The production budget is an important component of the operating budget, and the operating budget is an important part of the master budget. However, the production budget is not the foundation of the master budget. The foundation of the master budget is the budget from which all the other component budgets are derived.

Subtracting the fixed expenses of €45,000 gives a flexible budget operating income of €86,250.

3-3.q: All of the following would appear on a projected schedule of cost of goods manufactured except for
o: the cost of raw materials used.
3-3.e: Cost of Goods Manufactured is equal to Direct Materials Used + Direct Labor Used + Manufacturing Overhead Applied + Beginning Work-In-Process Inventory – Ending Work-InProcess Inventory. Therefore, ending work-in-process inventory, cost of raw materials used and applied manufacturing overhead would all appear on a projected schedule of cost of goods manufactured. Beginning finished goods inventory would not appear on a projected schedule of cost of goods manufactured.

3-3.q: Which one of the following is in a company’s cash budget?
3-3.e: The cash budget shows the planned sources and uses of cash for the budget period, including receipts and disbursements, and is generally segmented by quarter or by month. The receipt of cash expected for a sale of land would be an item included in the cash budget.

wrongWhy.0: Depreciation is a noncash transaction, so depreciation of plant equipment would not be in a company's cash budget.
wrongWhy.2: Conversion of debt for equity would not entail any cash transaction, so conversion of debt for equity would not be in a company's cash budget.
wrongWhy.3: Amortization is a noncash transaction, so amortization of patent costs would not be in a company's cash budget.

3-3.q: Many companies use comprehensive budgeting in planning for the next year's activities. When both an operating budget and a financial budget are prepared, which one of the following is correct concerning the financial budget? Included in the Financial Budget, respectively: Capital Budget, Master Budget Balance Sheet, Cash Budget
3-3.e: The financial budget includes the master budget balance sheet, the budgeted statement of cash flows, the cash budget, and the capital budget.

wrongWhy.1: The financial budget includes the master budget balance sheet, the budgeted statement of cash flows, the cash budget, and the capital budget. The capital budget shows where/how large amounts of capital will be utilized. The cash budget tracks the inflows and outflows of cash on a month-by-month (possibly even week-by-week or day-by-day) basis. The budgeted balance sheet shows how the entire budget is pulled together.
wrongWhy.2: The financial budget includes the master budget balance sheet, the budgeted statement of cash flows, the cash budget, and the capital budget. The balance sheet is an important part of the financial budget as it pulls everything together at the end.
wrongWhy.3: The financial budget includes the master budget balance sheet, the budgeted statement of cash flows, the cash budget, and the capital budget. The capital budget is necessary as it demonstrates where large capital expenditures will be made and how capital will be used. If capital expenditures for the coming year will affect the balance sheet, income statement and cash flows, these capital expenditures must be reflected in the budgeted balance sheet, income statement and cash flow statement, so the capital budget is required in order to develop these other budgets.
